paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1184 English Language — AI-authored Simplified Practice 27: The Kite (§B style, simplified band)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Secondary 2 girl who flies a kite — a gift from her late grandfather — on the field beside her HDB block, loses it when the line snaps, and learns to let go. Answer the questions that follow. Section B [15 marks] — AI-authored simplified practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1184/02 (formerly 1128) Section B format and calibrated to the SEAB assessment objectives (literal / inferential comprehension, vocabulary in context, language for effect), pitched at students stretching from middle secondary toward the full O-Level standard. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. Items mix mark values [1]/[2]: 7 short-answer questions (Q1-Q7) plus a 4-blank emotion-flowchart MCQ (Q8). Answer in your own words as far as possible.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
My grandfather made the kite for me the year before he passed away. It was a diamond of red paper stretched over thin bamboo sticks, with a long tail of folded cloth that he had cut from one of his old shirts. He used to take me to the field beside our block in Yishun to fly it, holding my small hands over his on the spool.

Paragraph 2
After he was gone, I kept the kite folded flat on top of my cupboard. For two years I did not fly it. I told myself the wind was never right, or that I was too busy with homework. The truth was simpler: I was afraid that if I flew it, something would tear, and I would lose the last thing his hands had touched.

Paragraph 3
On the first Sunday of the June holidays, the sky over Yishun was a clean, wide blue. My mother saw me looking at the cupboard and said gently, 'He made it to fly, you know. Not to keep in a box.' So I took it down, walked to the field where he used to bring me, and let out the line.

Paragraph 4
The kite climbed at once, as if it had been waiting all this time. It rose above the playground, above the lamp posts, above the highest flats, a small red shape against the blue. I laughed out loud. For a moment it felt as though my grandfather's hands were over mine again, steady on the spool.

Paragraph 5
Then a strong gust came across the field. The line went tight and hummed like a guitar string. I gripped the spool harder. There was a sharp snap, and suddenly the line in my hands was loose and weightless. The kite tipped, swung, and drifted away over the rooftops, growing smaller and smaller until I could no longer tell it from a bird.

Paragraph 6
I ran after it through the void deck and along the next block, my slippers slapping the concrete, but it was already gone. I stood there with the empty spool, my chest tight. I had done exactly what I feared. I had lost it. I sat down on a stone bench and did not move for a long time.

Paragraph 7
When I came home, my mother did not scold me. She sat beside me and said, 'You gave it back to the sky. That is where he flew it.' I thought about that. The kite was not in a box growing dusty and forgotten. It had flown as high as it could, the way he had made it to. Slowly, the tightness in my chest began to loosen.

Paragraph 8
I never found the kite. But that evening I took out a sheet of red paper and some bamboo from the market, and I began, clumsily, to fold and cut and tie. My fingers were not as clever as his. The new kite was not as neat. Yet as I worked, I understood at last that the gift was never the paper and the sticks. It was knowing how to make them — and how to let them go.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1184 §B style practice, simplified-band difficulty; not from a past exam. HDB: Housing and Development Board public-housing flats; void deck: the open ground floor of an HDB block; Yishun: a residential town in northern Singapore; spool: the reel that holds and releases the kite line.)

questions.stem: Q1. From Paragraph 1, what two materials did the grandfather use to make the kite itself (not the tail)? [1]
questions.answer: red paper and thin bamboo sticks

questions.stem: Q2. Using your own words, explain the real reason the narrator did not fly the kite for two years. (Paragraph 2) [2]
questions.answer: ① she was afraid it would tear or be lost; ② it was the last thing her grandfather's hands had touched, so losing it felt like losing him

questions.stem: Q3. What does the word 'climbed' (Paragraph 4) suggest about the way the kite rose into the sky? [1]
questions.answer: it went up quickly and steadily / easily, almost on its own

questions.stem: Q4. What is the effect of the comparison 'The line went tight and hummed like a guitar string' (Paragraph 5)? [2]
questions.answer: the simile makes the strain on the line vivid — you can almost hear it stretched to its limit, warning that it is about to snap

questions.stem: Q5. From Paragraph 6, why does the narrator say 'I had done exactly what I feared'? [1]
questions.answer: the kite had blown away and was lost — the very thing she had dreaded all along had happened

questions.stem: Q6. Using your own words, explain how her mother's words in Paragraph 7 changed the way the narrator felt. [2]
questions.answer: ① they helped her see the kite had flown as it was meant to, not sat forgotten in a box; ② so her sadness eased and she felt comforted / at peace

questions.stem: Q7. From Paragraph 8, what does the narrator finally realise her grandfather's real gift was? [2]
questions.answer: ① not the physical paper and sticks; ② but knowing how to make a kite — and learning how to let it go

sections.instruction: Q8. The narrator's main feeling shifts as the day with the kite unfolds. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best describes her dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: fearful / joyful / heartbroken / accepting / jealous / furious / bored / impatient. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'The Kite' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift as the day unfolds — from fearful in Paragraph 2, when she keeps the kite folded on the cupboard for two years because she dreads losing the last thing her grandfather touched, to joyful in Paragraph 4, when the kite climbs high above the flats and she laughs out loud and feels his hands over hers again, to heartbroken in Paragraph 6, when the line snaps, the kite drifts away over the rooftops, and she sits on a stone bench with the empty spool, ending accepting in Paragraphs 7 and 8, when her mother's words help her make peace with the loss and she begins to fold a new kite of her own. Pick the single best word from the bank for each moment.

questions.stem: Q8(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 2, when she keeps the kite folded flat on top of the cupboard for two years and tells herself the wind is never right, because she is afraid something will tear and she will lose the last thing her grandfather's hands had touched? [1]

Q8(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 4, when the kite climbs above the playground, the lamp posts and the highest flats, she laughs out loud, and for a moment it feels as though her grandfather's hands are steady over hers on the spool? [1]

Q8(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 6, when the line has snapped, the kite has drifted away over the rooftops out of sight, and she sits down alone on a stone bench with the empty spool and her chest tight, unable to move for a long time? [1]

Q8(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 7 and 8, when her mother's words help her see that the kite flew as high as it could, the tightness in her chest loosens, and that evening she calmly begins to fold and tie a new kite of her own? [1]
